english: stopper, stopple, also k'a-°dig (kha 'dig); *dig-c'e* (*dig che*) Ld. to put in a stopper; to stop up, to close with a stopper; *dig-ril* (*dig ril*) C. musket-ball. Cf. dig (dig).

pf. btiṅ (bting), fut. ydiṅ (gding), imp. tiṅ(s) (ting(s)), to spread on the ground, a mat, carpet etc.; to scatter, sprinkle, strew, grass or hay to lie upon, ashes on the snow etc.; *btiṅ-ba* (*bting ba*) sbst. W. a small carpet, on which the Lamas use to sit; *mal-btiṅ* (*mal bting*) C. bedding, pillow, or blanket. — °diṅ rgyáb-pa ('ding rgyab pa) Sch. to weigh in one's mind, to consider; to suspect, to entertain a suspicion.
meeting-house, house of assembly; c'os °dúd-pai (chos 'dud pa'i) (quasi) church, chapel Dzl.

pf. °dus ('dus), (vb. n. to sdúd-pa (sdud pa)) 1. to come together, to assemble, of men and animals; °dun-k'áṅ-du ('dun khang du) Dzl.; °dús-sam ma °dus ('dus sam ma 'dus) are they already assembled? daṅ (dang) with (a person) Tar.; in order to fight Stg.; of things: nyés-pa t'ams-c'ád dei lús-la °duo (nyes pa thams cad de'i lus la 'du'o), v. nyes-pa (nyes pa); °du-ba ('du ba) and °dus-pa ('dus pa) sbst. a coming together, an assembling, a gathering, esp. in Med. a (somewhat indefinite) disease, or cause of disease; °dus-sa ('dus sa) meeting-place Glr.; las-mí maṅ-po °dus-sa (las mi mang po 'dus sa) an establishment comprizing many workmen, manufactory, workshop, workhouse, *dzóm-°du yóṅ-gin dug* (*dzom 'du yong gin dug*) C. they flock or crowd together; ts'oṅ-°dus (tshong 'dus) the assembled traders or dealers, the market frq.; skyabs-kún-°dus (skyabs kun 'dus) 'a collection of all the refuges' is a name given to Milaraspa. — 2. to unite, to join one another, k'yo-s'úg-tu (khyo shug tu) as husband and wife, to get married; in a special sense in philosophical language: 1. to unite (opp. to brál-ba (bral ba)), e.g. the soul uniting with an organ of sense, like sdéb-pa (sdeb pa), Mil. 2. °dus-byás ('dus byas) composed of two or more ingredients, °dus-ma-byas ('dus ma byas) consisting of one thing, simple, elementary; only this is eternal, every thing compounded is perishable, frq. — 3. to be pressed or crowded together, *s'ril dús-te dug* (*shril dus te dug*) Ld. they stand crowded, in serried files or ranks; intellectually: dam-c'os °dus-pa (dam chos 'dus pa) a compressed system of religion. — 4. °dús-pa ('dus pa) to consist of or in, gnyís-su °dus-so (gnyis su 'dus so) (religion) consists of two things Thgy.; snaṅ-srid séms-su °dús-te gda (snang srid sems su 'dus te gda) the external world consists of spirit, is spirit, i.e. is nothing Mil. — 5. col.: to be drawn together, to contract, to shrink, *dus c'a dug* (*dus cha dug*) Ld. it shrinks, e.g. wood or paper from heat; *tsa-°dú* (*tsa 'du*) C. prob. cramp, spasm, convulsion; *dús-k'aṅ* (*dus khang*) Ld. elastic, springy.

Ssk. संस्कार, (the Tibetan word is nothing but a literal translation of the Ssk. saṅskāra; cf. also °du-s'és ('du shes) and p'uṅ-po (phung po)) 'one of the obscurest and most difficult terms of Buddhist philosophy' Köpp. I, 603, where the various translations are enumerated that have been attempted, such as: idea, notion, imagination (cf. Burn. I, 503), action (Was.) etc. It should, however, at once be acknowledged, that the word cannot be translated into a European language, as the meaning given to it is not the result of honest research and observation, but a product of arbitrary and wild speculation.

noise, bustle, din, clamour, °du-°dzi méd-pai dbén-pa ('du 'dzi med pa'i dben pa) a solitude without any noise Mil.; °du-°dzi-la gnás-pa ('du 'dzi la gnas pa) to live in the midst of the bustle of worldly affairs; °du-ziṅ ('du zing), °du-lóṅ ('du long) Cs. id.

Ssk. संज्ञा ('con-scientia') corresponds in most cases to our idea, notion, conception, image, although sometimes perception, feeling, sense, thought, consciousness may be employed for it: nór-la rtág-tu yód-pai °du-s'és skyéd-pa (nor la rtag tu yod pa'i 'du shes skyed pa) to combine with earthly goods the idea of constant possession S.O. and thus frq.; lús-la grui °du-s'és júg-pa (lus la gru'i 'du shes 'jug pa) to unite with the human body the idea of a ship, to represent the body as a ship, Thgy.; skyó-bai °du-s'és byuṅ (skyo ba'i 'du shes byung) the perception, the feeling of discomfort arises S.g.; k'ró-bai °du-s'és-spáṅ-ba (khro ba'i 'du shes spang ba) to detest the idea, the thought of anger Dzl.; dgé-bai p'yógs-la °du-s'és c'úṅ-zad kyaṅ ma gyos (dge ba'i phyogs la 'du shes cung zad kyang ma gyos) no thoughts, no inclinations, tending to virtue, arose (in him), virtuous emotions never stirred in his mind; c'ágs-pai (chags pa'i)
